title: Git and Version Control for Music Therapy
description: Learn Git and version control through the lens of music therapy practice, managing session plans, collaborating with therapists, and maintaining patient care documentation.

nodes.title: Git Basics: Version Control for Your Therapy Sessions
nodes.content: Git is like a recording system for your music therapy sessions. Just as a therapist keeps detailed notes of each session's progress and modifications, Git tracks every change you make to your files. In music therapy, you might have multiple versions of a patient's session plan—the original, one with added breathing exercises, another with different instruments. Git helps you manage all these versions, understand what changed and why, and ensures you never lose important work. Version control is the backbone of professional therapy documentation.

nodes.title: Git Basics Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of Git fundamentals in the context of music therapy practice.

questions.question: Why is version control essential in music therapy practice?
options: It helps track changes to session plans and patient progress
It makes music files smaller
It improves the quality of recorded sound
It allows therapists to hear sessions remotely
feedback: Correct! Version control is crucial for maintaining detailed records of therapy evolution.
Incorrect. Version control focuses on change tracking, not file compression.
Incorrect. Quality depends on equipment and recording settings, not version control.
Incorrect. While sharing is possible, that's not the primary purpose of Git.
questions.explanation: Version control exists to preserve history and document change—essential for clinical and therapeutic accountability.

questions.question: What does a Git repository store?
options: Only the final version of your session plans
Only deleted or archived files
The complete history of all changes to your files
Encrypted patient medical records
feedback: Incorrect. Repositories preserve all versions, not just the latest.
Incorrect. Repositories keep all changes, including deleted ones.
Correct! Every change is recorded, allowing you to revisit any point in your therapy documentation.
Incorrect. Git stores code and documents, but patient records are typically in separate HIPAA-compliant systems.
questions.explanation: A repository is a complete archive of your project's history, enabling you to understand exactly how your therapy approaches evolved.

questions.question: Which best describes a commit in the context of a therapy session documentation?
options: A backup copy of a file sent to cloud storage
A snapshot of changes at a specific point in time with a message explaining why
Saving a music file to your computer
Recording a live therapy session with a patient
feedback: Incorrect. A commit is not just a backup—it's a tracked change with context.
Correct! Each commit is a meaningful checkpoint, documenting what changed and why, like noting therapy progress.
Incorrect. Saving a music file is a local action; commits are tracked changes in version control.
Incorrect. Recording sessions is documentation, but commits are about tracking file changes.
questions.explanation: Each commit is a moment in time where you've documented a meaningful change in your therapy documentation—essential for accountability.

nodes.title: Git Basics Practice: Committing Your Therapy Documentation
nodes.content: Apply Git basics by working through realistic scenarios from music therapy practice.

questions.question: You've updated a patient's session plan to include a new breathing exercise combined with guitar playing. What should you do next?
options: Create a commit with a message like 'Updated session plan: Added breathing exercise + guitar integration for relaxation'
Save the file and wait a few days before making any changes
Delete the old version to save storage space
Email the changes to other therapists without documenting them
feedback: Correct! A clear commit message documents why you made the change—essential for tracking your therapy evolution.
Incorrect. You should commit changes promptly with clear messages about what changed and why.
Incorrect. Version control keeps all versions, so you can revert if needed.
Incorrect. Email doesn't create documented, auditable records like Git commits do.
questions.explanation: Committing with clear messages ensures other therapists and your future self understand the rationale behind session plan modifications.

questions.question: A colleague asks: 'When was the breathing exercise added to the relaxation protocol?' How does Git help you answer this?
options: You can check the commit history to see exactly when the change was made and why
You have to ask the colleague who wrote it
You check the most recent version of the file
You look at backup files from different dates
feedback: Correct! Git's history preserves when changes were made and their commit messages explain why.
Incorrect. With version control, you have a definitive record without needing to ask.
Incorrect. The current version doesn't show history or timing.
Incorrect. While backups might exist, Git provides a much clearer, timestamped history.
questions.explanation: Git commits create an auditable timeline of your therapy documentation, enabling accountability and informed collaboration.

questions.question: You've made edits to three different patient session plans. Should you commit them together or separately?
options: Commit them all together to save time
Commit each patient's changes separately, with messages explaining what improved in their protocol
Don't commit until all patients are done with their sessions
Commit only the most important changes
feedback: Incorrect. Bundling unrelated changes makes history harder to understand and revert selectively.
Correct! Separate commits for each logical unit (patient) keep your history clear and meaningful.
Incorrect. Commit regularly to preserve progress; don't wait for sessions to end.
Incorrect. Commit all meaningful changes—Git preserves all of them.
questions.explanation: Logical, separated commits make your version history readable and allow you to understand the evolution of each patient's therapy.

nodes.title: Branching: Creating Parallel Therapy Approaches
nodes.content: In music therapy, you often want to try different approaches without affecting your proven main treatment plan. Git branching lets you create a parallel version—like having one main session plan that's proven effective with your patient population, and a feature branch where you experiment with new techniques like neurologic music therapy or rhythm-based interventions. When multiple therapists collaborate on a shared protocol, each can create their own branch for their proposed improvements. Once thoroughly tested, your experimental branch merges back into the main branch, ensuring all team members benefit from validated innovations. This is essential when building comprehensive therapy protocols that evolve based on clinical evidence.

nodes.title: Branching and Collaboration Assessment
nodes.content: Assess your understanding of Git branching in collaborative music therapy environments.

questions.question: Your team of three therapists is developing an enhanced protocol for trauma-informed music therapy. What's the best way to use Git branching?
options: Everyone commits directly to the main branch to keep everything synchronized
Each therapist creates their own feature branch, then merges into main after team review
One therapist maintains control and others send suggestions via email
Create one branch per therapist and never merge—keep protocols separate
feedback: Incorrect. Direct commits to main risk merging incomplete or conflicting work.
Correct! Feature branches allow parallel work and enable peer review before integration.
Incorrect. Email-based workflows lack version control and accountability.
Incorrect. Keeping protocols separate defeats the purpose of collaboration.
questions.explanation: Feature branches enable your team to innovate independently while maintaining a stable, reviewed main branch that represents your validated therapy protocol.

questions.question: A therapist creates a feature branch called 'improve-stress-reduction-protocol.' They've made great improvements. What happens next?
options: The branch sits idle until the therapist remembers to update main manually
Automatic merge—no review needed
Team reviews the changes, discusses clinical outcomes, then merges into main
The branch is deleted and all changes are lost
feedback: Incorrect. Valuable work shouldn't be left uncommitted; it should be integrated.
Incorrect. Clinical improvements should be reviewed by the team before adoption.
Correct! Peer review ensures quality and validates improvements before they become standard practice.
Incorrect. Deleting would waste the work; Git preserves all commits.
questions.explanation: Merging with review maintains clinical rigor and ensures team confidence in protocol changes before they're adopted practice-wide.

questions.question: Two therapists both modify the opening breathing exercise in the session template. How does Git help prevent chaos?
options: It automatically picks one version and discards the other without asking
It prevents both changes and requires you to start over
It preserves both changes separately and alerts you to the conflict for manual resolution
It only works if therapists coordinate perfectly, so conflicts never happen
feedback: Incorrect. Git doesn't silently discard work; it flags conflicts for human resolution.
Incorrect. Conflicts don't prevent merging; they require thoughtful resolution.
Correct! Git shows you both edits so your team can discuss and decide on the best version.
Incorrect. Conflicts happen naturally; Git tools help resolve them transparently.
questions.explanation: Merge conflicts are opportunities for your team to discuss clinical approaches and choose the best combined version of your protocol.

nodes.title: Branching Practice: Managing Multiple Protocol Improvements
nodes.content: Practice creating, updating, and merging branches in realistic music therapy scenarios.

questions.question: You want to test a new approach using neurologic music therapy for stroke recovery patients. Which Git commands in order?
options: git branch neuro-stroke, git checkout neuro-stroke, make changes, git commit
git commit, git merge, git branch
git merge, git checkout, git branch
git delete, git create, git branch
feedback: Correct! Create the branch, switch to it, make changes, and commit—preserving your main protocol intact.
Incorrect. You must branch first, then commit to that branch.
Incorrect. That sequence doesn't create a working branch.
Incorrect. Those aren't the right commands or sequence.
questions.explanation: Creating a branch first protects your main therapy protocol while you experiment with neurologic music therapy techniques.

questions.question: After testing your neurologic music therapy branch for two weeks with positive results, what should happen?
options: Keep it as a separate branch forever—the protocol is now frozen
Merge the feature branch into main, allowing all therapists to adopt the validated approach
Delete the branch without merging to save storage space
Email the results to colleagues without using Git
feedback: Incorrect. Proven improvements should be integrated into main, not left isolated.
Correct! Merging validated innovations into main ensures your entire team benefits.
Incorrect. Git commits are permanent; deleting branches doesn't free space and loses access.
Incorrect. Email doesn't create permanent, version-controlled documentation.
questions.explanation: Merging validated protocols amplifies their benefit across your entire therapy team.

questions.question: You realize the neurologic music therapy branch needs updates while your colleague is working on a different branch. Can you both work simultaneously?
options: No—only one person can work at a time in Git
Yes—branching allows parallel work; you each commit to your respective branches
No—branches conflict immediately
Only if you're working on the exact same files
feedback: Incorrect. Branching specifically enables parallel work.
Correct! Multiple branches allow teams to innovate in parallel without stepping on each other.
Incorrect. Branches isolate work until intentionally merged.
Incorrect. Different branches work on any files independently.
questions.explanation: Branching's power lies in enabling parallel development—your team scales efficiently while maintaining stability.

nodes.title: Advanced Git Workflows: Managing Complex Therapy Projects
nodes.content: As your music therapy practice grows with multiple therapists, diverse patient populations, and evolving clinical protocols, you need advanced Git techniques. Rebasing keeps your commit history clean and linear—when integrating multiple therapists' contributions, rebasing ensures a readable timeline of improvements rather than tangled merge commits. Tags mark significant milestones: completing a 12-week protocol, finalizing a trauma-informed approach, or documenting evidence-based practices. Stashing lets you pause work on an experimental technique mid-development to handle urgent patient care, then resume exactly where you left off. Cherry-picking applies a specific successful intervention from one patient's protocol to another's without merging entire branches. These tools keep complex, multi-therapist projects organized, ensure nothing falls through the cracks, and enable sophisticated clinical documentation that supports both individual patient care and evidence-based practice development.

nodes.title: Advanced Git Workflows Assessment
nodes.content: Demonstrate mastery of advanced Git techniques in professional music therapy contexts.

questions.question: You've committed work to your trauma-informed protocol branch, but your team's main branch has evolved. What does rebasing do?
options: It permanently deletes your commits
It replays your commits on top of the latest main, creating a clean linear history
It merges everything and creates a large merge commit
It prevents you from updating your branch
feedback: Incorrect. Rebasing preserves your work; it just reorganizes it.
Correct! Rebasing ensures your team's contribution integrates cleanly into the evolving main protocol.
Incorrect. That's a merge strategy, not rebasing.
Incorrect. Rebasing updates your branch to reflect recent changes.
questions.explanation: Rebasing maintains a clean, linear history—essential when your therapy team commits improvements in parallel.

questions.question: You've reached a major milestone: your 6-week intensive music therapy curriculum is clinically validated and stable. What Git feature documents this?
options: A commit message that mentions the milestone
A Git tag marking this specific commit as 'v1.0-validated-curriculum'
A backup file named 'final-version'
An email announcement to your team
feedback: Incorrect. Commit messages document changes, not permanent milestone markers.
Correct! Tags create permanent, easily referenced points in history.
Incorrect. Backups are informal and easily lost or confused.
Incorrect. Email lacks version control permanence.
questions.explanation: Tags create searchable, permanent markers in your project history—allowing you to instantly reference 'the validated v1.0 curriculum' months or years later.

questions.question: During a session, a therapist realizes they need to pause work on a new singing meditation technique to handle a crisis patient intervention. They haven't committed yet. What's the best approach?
options: Delete the unfinished work and start over later
Commit the incomplete work with a 'WIP' message
Use git stash to save the work-in-progress, handle the patient need, then unstash to resume
Email the incomplete files to a colleague
feedback: Incorrect. You'd lose work and context.
Incorrect. Committing incomplete work clutters your history.
Correct! Stashing preserves your work in a safe state while freeing your working directory.
Incorrect. Email doesn't integrate with your version control.
questions.explanation: Stashing bridges the gap between version control and real-world clinical interruptions—your work is safe and you can resume with full context.

nodes.title: Git Mastery for Music Therapy: Complete!
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've successfully mastered Git and version control through the lens of music therapy practice. You now understand how to track the evolution of your therapy protocols, collaborate with colleagues using branching workflows, resolve conflicts thoughtfully, manage complex multi-therapist projects with advanced techniques, and maintain an auditable history of your clinical documentation. These skills will enhance your professional practice, support evidence-based protocol development, enable seamless team collaboration, and ensure nothing important is ever lost. Apply Git to your therapy documentation, and watch your practice become more organized, collaborative, and evidence-driven.
